result:
I keep thinking about the moment when a word loses its meaning.  
You say your own name out loud thirty times in a row and suddenly it’s just a shape of air, a percussion in your mouth, something a crow might cough up.  
That’s the hinge I want to live on: the place where language slips off the world like wet silk and we’re left staring at the naked yes or no underneath.

Yesterday I watched a woman on the subway unwrap a sandwich as if it were a newborn—peeling back the wax paper with the same hush you give fragile wings.  
She caught me looking and for a second we shared the secret that nothing is ordinary when someone else is watching.  
After she left, the air kept the shape of her reverence; the whole car smelled of rye and miracle.

I used to believe the opposite of forgetting is memory, but now I think it’s astonishment.  
Memory is a Polaroid you shake until the colors behave; astonishment is the shutter you never heard click in the first place.  
One keeps the past, the other keeps you from ever believing the past is done with you.

There’s a small park near my apartment where the city planted gingkos in identical square plots of dirt.  
Every fall they drop their vomit-smelling fruit at the exact same hour, as if they rehearsed all year for this synchronized disgust.  
I go there anyway because the older woman who feeds the pigeons calls them her “little umbrellas” and because the teenagers practicing skateboard tricks have started greeting me like I’m a landmark—  
“Hey, bench guy!”—  
and landmarks don’t have to be useful; they only have to stay.

At night the traffic lights turn the empty intersection into a private planet where red and green are just two moods of the same deity.  
I stand on the corner, press the beg button like a prayer wheel, and feel the city decide whether I’m allowed to cross.  
Permission is a strange religion, but we all worship it with our shoes.

Inside my apartment I keep a glass of water that’s been there so long it’s become an heirloom.  
I don’t drink it; I just top it off when evaporation lowers the level, a routine like tending a very quiet fire.  
Some evenings I swear the glass is clouded with everything I almost said that day, sentences condensed on the rim like lipstick ghosts.

Maybe that’s the real autobiography: not the diary you burn, not the tweets archived in some Arctic vault, but the slow mineral accretion of almosts on ordinary silica.  
Future archaeologists will scrape the film, hold it to the light and see a single frame of hesitation—  
my mouth open, the word “stay” still swinging on the rope of the tongue, the moment before the hinge, before the silk hits the floor.
